metadata.raw_response_text:
# The Weight of Every Case: A Public Defender's Day

The file folders are stacked in two leaning towers on the corner of Maya Reyes's desk, and she has already apologized for them twice before sitting down. It is 7:42 in the morning. Court starts at nine.

"I used to organize them alphabetically," she says, pulling one from the middle of a stack with the practiced confidence of someone who has learned which cards the tower will forgive. "Now I organize them by who's incarcerated. Those people are on a clock."

Reyes is 34 and has worked as a public defender in the Allegheny County Public Defender's Office — Pittsburgh, population 300,000, neither the largest nor smallest stage for this kind of work — for six years. She currently carries 112 open cases. The American Bar Association recommends that a public defender handle no more than 150 felonies per year. Reyes passed that number in August, and it is November.

---

Public defenders occupy one of the stranger positions in American civic life. They are constitutionally guaranteed — *Gideon v. Wainwright*, 1963, established that the Sixth Amendment requires the state to provide counsel to defendants who cannot afford one — and chronically underfunded. They are often the most experienced attorneys in a courtroom, handling more trials in a year than many private lawyers see in a decade, and they are routinely dismissed, in ways both subtle and overt, as the legal system's second tier.

Reyes does not spend much energy on the perception. She has 112 cases.

"People ask me how I do it," she says. "I think they're asking an existential question, like how do I live with myself. But the actual answer is logistical. You triage. You build systems. You sleep when you can."

Her systems are visible throughout the office, a floor of a county building with fluorescent lighting and carpet that might once have been green. She has a color-coded calendar on one monitor, a client-tracking spreadsheet on the other. She keeps a notebook with the names of incarcerated clients and their bail amounts — not because she doesn't know the numbers, but because writing them down, she says, reminds her they're people waiting on her, not files waiting on her.

---

The morning's docket has four of her clients on it. One is a drug possession case she expects to resolve with a plea — a young man, 22, first offense, and she spent two days negotiating a diversion program that will keep a conviction off his record. She explains this without pride, the way a plumber explains fixing a pipe. It is what needed to happen.

Another case she is less settled about. Her client is a 47-year-old man charged with aggravated assault after a fight outside a bar. Reyes believes the charge is overreaching — the complaining witness has a history with her client that the police report omits, and the surveillance footage, which she requested in September, has still not been fully disclosed by the prosecution.

"In a private firm, someone would have filed three motions and made two phone calls and that footage would have materialized," she says, not bitterly, as observation. "I filed the motion. The calls are harder when you're calling about eleven different things the same week."

She does not blame prosecutors in general, she is careful to say. Several of the ADAs she appears against regularly are professional and fair. The problem is structural — a funding imbalance that means the office investigating and charging a crime has more resources than the office defending against it. Pittsburgh is not unusual in this. A 2022 report from the Brennan Center for Justice found that public defenders in most jurisdictions are funded at roughly half the rate of prosecutors' offices.

---

By 10:15 the diversion agreement is signed. Her client shakes her hand and she holds it for a moment — she is deliberate about this, she says later, about letting people feel that the interaction was real — and then he is gone and she is already looking at her phone, where an investigator has texted about a witness in a robbery case set for trial in three weeks.

Between the morning session and the afternoon calendar, she eats a sandwich at her desk and takes a call from a client's mother, who wants to understand why the case is taking so long. Reyes explains, again, the mechanics of discovery and scheduling. She is patient. She has had this conversation many hundreds of times, and she knows it is never really about discovery. It is about a family watching someone they love move through a system that seems to operate on its own clock, indifferent to theirs.

"I got into this because of a professor who said public defense was where constitutional law actually lives," she says after she hangs up. "Like, the Bill of Rights isn't abstract here. Every day I'm touching the Fourth Amendment, the Sixth Amendment. It matters what I do. That's still true. I haven't talked myself out of that."

She pauses.

"But I also know that last week I had a client plead guilty to something I think we could have fought, and I think part of why he agreed to the plea is that he could see how stretched I was. He didn't want to be a burden. A 19-year-old kid didn't want to burden his public defender." She straightens some papers. "That's the thing I carry."

---

The afternoon brings a bail hearing for a client who has been in the county jail for 61 days awaiting trial on a theft charge. The original bail was set at $10,000 — a figure her client, who works part-time at a warehouse, cannot meet. Reyes has filed for reduction. She presents the judge with documentation: her client's employment record, a letter from his landlord, his lack of prior convictions, the fact that he appeared for every required appointment before his arrest.

The judge reduces bail to $2,500. Her client, watching by video from the jail, looks like a man who has received news he was afraid to hope for.

Walking back to her office, Reyes says this is one of the parts that still feels clean. "A person is less free than they should be. You make an argument. They become more free. The logic is direct."

She will spend the next two hours returning phone calls, reviewing evidence in the robbery case, and drafting a suppression motion arguing that her client's traffic stop — and the subsequent search that produced the prosecution's main evidence — was constitutionally defective. It is detailed, careful work, and she does it in a shared office with two other defenders at adjacent desks, phones occasionally ringing, someone's police scanner murmuring in the background.

---

At six o'clock she is still at her desk. The file towers have been disturbed and reconstituted in slightly different shapes.

She is asked whether she thinks the system is broken.

"Broken implies it worked once and something went wrong," she says. "I think it was built with this baked in — the idea that poor defendants would get just enough representation to satisfy the constitutional requirement. Not enough to actually equalize anything." She clicks off one of her monitors. "My job is to fight inside that. I don't know if that makes me complicit or useful. Most days I decide it makes me useful and move on, because the alternative is paralysis and my clients can't afford my paralysis."

She picks up the notebook with the names of her incarcerated clients and puts it in her bag, the way another person might take home a novel they haven't finished.

Tomorrow her first hearing is at eight-thirty.
